text: The right ureter is visualized crossing the right iliac artery. The assistant retracts the mesosigmoid ventrally, and a peritoneal incision is made over the sacral promontory. The sacral hollow is exposed for subsequent mesh fixation using blunt and sharp dissection.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the right hypogastric nerve. The rectovaginal septum is opened, and dissection proceeds between the rectum and the posterior vaginal wall. The lateral ligament is preserved to prevent recurrence and reduce constipation. The rectovaginal septum is opened broadly down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision caudally into an inverted J shape along the right side of the rectum, curving it to the left over the ventral aspect of the rectum and the pouch of Douglas. A strip of prolene mesh, measuring three by fifteen centimeters, is inserted. Temporary hysteropexy is achieved with transparietal sutures, suspending the uterus to the abdominal wall using non-absorbable sutures. The mesh is then sutured to the ventral aspect on both sides of the distal rectum.

The mesh is positioned to reinforce the rectovaginal septum and is fixed onto the sacral promontory using a tacker. The surgeon then closes the lateral borders of the incised peritoneum over the mesh with resorbable sutures.
